titleStmt.title: Automated Extraction of Character Features in Fiction: Comparing Bert-based Models and Large Language Models on Fanfiction in English and Chinese

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon
Match where technical expertise is helpful.

div.head: 1 Introduction
div.p: Fanfiction is a unique form of participatory storytelling where fans reinterpret and expand on existing narratives (Jenkins 2012). It presents a distinct avenue for literary and cultural research due to its participatory nature and its adaptive portrayal of canonical narratives (Milli / Bamman 2016).

Character analysis in fiction often focuses on personas or relationships, e.g., graphical model to infer latent character personas and group character traits by Bamman et al. (2013), character personality prediction by Flekova and Gurevych (2015), unsupervised neural network for relationships evolution by Iyyer et al. (2016), while recent advancements, such as Papoudakis et al. (2024) constructed BOOKWORM dataset of pairing character description and analysis, enable LLMs for narrative understanding.

In the context of fanfiction, computational methods have been employed to explore character adaptation, interaction, and recontextualization across fandoms (Yin et al. 2017). Although studies have examined certain factors influencing fanfiction’s popularity, such as innovation (Jing et al. 2019) and character frequency ( Nguyen et al. 2024), research has yet to examine characterization within fanfiction systematically. However, the character lies at the center of the fanfiction narrative. The GOLEM database (Pannach et al. 2024) contains 8 million stories and related metadata from Archive of Our Own (AO3) (Fiesler, et al. 2016), one of the largest fanfiction platforms, spanning from 2000 to September 2022. AO3 enables fanfiction authors to categorize their works using a well-established tag system. Among the 200 most-used tags, 106 related to character features, e.g., 'Aged-Up Character(s) ' reflecting modifications to canonical biographical information, 'First Kiss' denoting specific behaviors, and 'Grief/Mourning' capturing the emotional and psychological depth of characters. Therefore, systematically examining these character features is essential for understanding diverse aspects of characterization.

div.head: 2 Methodology
div.p: To uncover cross-cultural narrative patterns of characters, this research addresses two primary questions: how NLP techniques can be applied to analyze fanfiction at scale to uncover cross-cultural narrative patterns? More specifically, how well do the existing Bert-based NLP pipelines perform on character coreference resolution, features, and dialogue extractions for both English and Chinese fanfiction? Can LLMs be used to improve the accuracy of this automated extraction?

The first step is defining character concepts and character features. We use the GOLEM ontology (Pianzola et al. 2025) that adopts a bottom-up approach to construct character features control vocabulary from AO3 character-related tags with the addition of structured information from multiple fan crowdsource databases. It includes biographical details (e.g. age, gender, occupation), physical descriptions (e.g. height, eye color, clothing), and psychological features (e.g. personality).

Two annotators will annotate 20 fanfictions (10 each in English and Chinese) across four fandoms, ensuring linguistic and cultural diversity. Annotation guidelines are grounded in the GOLEM ontology and controlled vocabulary, focusing on coreference resolution and the detailed representation of character traits in text. Since this is a pilot study for a bigger future annotation, the corpus is insufficient for model fine-tuning but still serves as a good reference for evaluating NLP models in the latter steps.

The framework integrates character recognition, coreference resolution, entity and dialogue extraction and linking, comparing Bert based NLP tools and LLMs. To explore the evolution of characters’ traits, this research introduces an automated pipeline for extracting character features from fanfiction in English and Chinese.

Then character recognition is the first step. BookNLP (Bamman 2021) and ARMCC (Song / Ying, 2024) will be evaluated for their ability to identify characters in English and Chinese texts, respectively. Coreference resolution links mentions of the same character throughout a narrative, a task complicated by fanfiction's abundance of pronouns in comparison to other types of texts (Yang / Pianzola 2024). We will also assess the performance of traditional Bert-based models alongside LLMs including Llama 3.1-70b (Grattafiori et al. 2025), Deepseek r1-70b (Guo et al. 2025) and the fine-tuned t5-3b model (Hicke / Minno, 2024) for their few shots capabilities on literary coreference annotation by testing on this small annotated dataset.

The second step is character feature extraction. Due to the lack of multilingual pipelines capable of performing this task, we draw inspiration from the methods of Flekova and Gurevych (2015). Based on previous character identification and coreference resolution, we decompose the task into three sub-tasks: dialogue extraction, explicit predicative extraction, and implicit character personality prediction on verb-based context extraction.

For dialogue extraction, dialogues are central to the construction of fictional characters (Koivisto / Nykänen 2016), as they often reveal implicit traits such as determination or kindness, providing a nuanced lens for character analysis. We will use BookNLP and ARMCC for dialogue extraction and speaker attribution.

For predicatives and verbs, we will adapt dependency parse trees to identify character-related attributes. Explicit traits are extracted from sentences containing character mentions and copulas (e.g. "X is brave") (Zhang et al. 2019). Implicit traits are inferred from contexts where characters appear as subjects (NSUBJ) or direct objects (DOBJ) in action-related sentences.

Explicit character traits in English texts will be identified using BookNLP, while for Chinese texts, we will utilize the Lexical Analysis of Chinese (LAC) model (Jiao et al. 2018), which integrates tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, and dependency parsing. For contexts with implicit traits, we will employ available personality prediction classifiers based on text, to extract nuanced psychological character traits based on contexts.

Finally, evaluating these results on human annotations alongside LLMs, we aim to identify how the current NLP tools perform in identifying biographical, physical, and psychological character traits for fiction and the directions for future improvement.

div.head: 3 Expected Contributions
div.p: By implementing and evaluating traditional models and LLMs, this research will provide insights into the performance of these tools in character-centric tasks, particularly in linguistically diverse datasets. The integration of recognition, coreference resolution, dialogue attribution, and trait extraction facilitates a holistic understanding of fictional characters. By combining annotation with NLP tools, this research aims to facilitate future research of characterization dynamics in global fanfiction, enabling cross-cultural comparisons of character adaptation and reader engagement.
